title: Effects of interface regularity on the bulk-edge correspondence in continuum photonic systems
abstract: In this study we analyze the topological invariants and edge states of transverse magnetic wave propagation in continuum photonic systems at a finite-width interface between two gyrotropic matrials with different magnetic bias. Where previous studies have almost exclusively considered sharp transitions between two different electromagnetic media, we consider the more general geometry where the magnetic field bias is allowed to vary arbitrarily in a finite-width interface between to bulk regions. We find that when the magnetic field bias varies continuously between the two bulk regions, the Bulk Edge Correspondence (BEC) holds robustly with respect to well-defined Chern invariants. However, discontinuities in the magnetic field bias introduce edge modes which are highly localized at the associated discontinuity and whose spectral properties alter the BEC. We analyze the spectral properties of these edge modes and define a new anomalous BEC in continuum photonic systems which includes contributions from topological invariants and discontinuities in magnetic field bias.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the standard Bulk-Edge Correspondence in photonic systems is sensitive to whether the interface between media is smooth or sharp. It introduces a new 'anomalable BEC' to account for edge modes that arise specifically from discontinuities in the magnetic field bias.
main_problem: Investigating how the regularity (smoothness vs. discontinuity) of an interface between two different magnetic media affects the Bulk-Edge Correspondence (BEC) and the emergence of edge modes in continuum photonic systems.
main_result: The authors find that while continuous interfaces preserve the standard BEC, discontinuities in the magnetic field bias introduce anomalous edge modes that alter the spectral flow, necessitating a new 'anomalous BEC' framework.
method: The study uses analytical derivations of asymptotic dispersion relations, spectral analysis of self-adjoint Hamiltonians, and numerical ODE-based methods to compare BDI and spatial cutoff regularization schemes.
model_or_system: The system consists of transverse magnetic (TM) wave propagation in continuum photonic systems, specifically modeled as light-matter interacting cold plasmas (gyrotropic media) with varying magnetic field bias.
key_observables: Chern numbers, Bulk-Difference Invariants (BDIs), edge state spectral flow, and edge current (asymmetric transport).
important_parameters: Magnetic field bias (cyclotron frequency), plasma frequency, interface regularity (continuous vs. discontinuous), and wavenumber (k).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the interface between two cold plasmas to avoid complex boundary conditions and assumes the system can be modeled via local Drude or linearized hydrodynamic approximations.
figures_summary: Figures show the energy spectrum for different interface profiles, demonstrating how discontinuities create new spectral branches and how regularization affects the stability of edge modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of interface regularity, presents the local Drude and hydrodynamic models, applies regularization techniques (BDI and SC), analyzes the spectral properties of edge modes, and concludes with a new anomalous BEC framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Spectrum of HI(kx) with Ω(y) which transitions smoothly between ΩS = −1 and ΩN = 1 with ωp = 2. Left shows local Drude model while right the hydrodynamic model discussed in Section V. In both cases the BEC holds using well-defined BDI’s (C1, C2) = (0, 2), although for the hydrodynamic model there is no longer an upper band gap.

FIG. 2: Spectrum of HI(kx) for which Ω(y) varies linearly between Ω(y ≤−1) = ΩS = −1 and Ω(y ≥1) = ΩN = 1 and has a single single discontinuity at y = 0. Black regions correspond to the bulk spectrum and each color represents the edge spectrum for a different value of Ωj. The half-jump size Ωj was varied from 0 to 1 at y = 0 with Ω(y) shown on the right. ωp = 2, kx, ωp, Ωin units of THz and y in units of c · 10−12 = 300µm.

FIG. 3: Spectrum of HI(kx) for which Ω(y) varies linearly between Ω(y ≤−1) = ΩS = −1 and Ω(y ≥1) = ΩN = 1 and has a single single discontinuity at y = 0. Ω(y), shown at the right, is the same as Figure 2 except for values of Ωj ≤0. Black regions again correspond to bulk spectrum with each color representing the edge spectrum for a given value of |Ωj|. ωp = 2, kx, ωp, Ωin units of THz and y in units of c · 10−12 = 300µm.

FIG. 4: Spectrum of HI(kx) for a non-linear transition of Ω(y) (shown at right) which contains two discontinuities, one positive and one negative, and one of which is not symmetric about y = 0. The spectral flow is now modified by both JiR and JiL, i ∈{1, 3}. For the first jump Ω(1) −= −0.4, Ω(1) + = 0.8 which yields ˆω(1) e = 0.5941 and ˆω(3) e = 2.3356. For the second jump Ω(2) −= −1 and Ω(2) + = 1 yielding ˆω(1) e = 1 and ˆω(3) e = 2.5616 = E2. Therefore the blue shaded region now has a spectral flow of -1, yellow +2, and red +3.

FIG. 5: Numerically calculated spectra for local model with BDI regularization (13) applied with ωp = 2 and kc = 5. Ω(y) is a linear transition between ΩS = −1 and ΩN = 1 with a jump at y = 0 as in Figures 2 and 3. Left models positive jumps and right negative jumps.
